RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 10-[3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propyl]-2-(trifluoromethyl)phenothiazine, also referred to as Trifluoperazine, a phenothiazine antipsychotic drug that primarily acts as a dopamine antagonist, is based on the phenothiazine tricyclic core, features an electron-withdrawing trifluoromethyl group at the 2-position which enhances its lipophilicity, and has a propyl chain connecting an N-methylpiperazine group at the 10-position nitrogen; structurally, this is characteristic of many classic antipsychotic drugs, and this compound is expected to show potent dopamine receptor antagonist activity, particularly towards the central nervous system D2 receptor, making it potentially useful for treating schizophrenia or for its sedative effects.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Kaempferol, is a natural flavonol compound structurally similar to Quercetin (2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-3,5,7-trihydroxychromen-4-one) and Myricetin (3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one), differing mainly in the number of hydroxyl groups on the B ring, having only one at position 4, with the formula C15H10O6. Kaempferol is found in a variety of plant foods, such as tea, broccoli, grapefruit, and herbs. Functionally, as a dietary flavonol, Kaempferol possesses significant antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and potential anticancer properties, it modulates cell signaling pathways (e.g., PI3K/Akt) and is studied for cardiovascular and neurodegenerative disease prevention, research also suggests it can regulate apoptosis, inhibit angiogenesis, and suppress cell proliferation, thereby potentially inhibiting tumor development, its role in the prevention of chronic diseases and the maintenance of cardiovascular health is particularly studied, although its bioactivity may differ from other flavonols due to the single hydroxyl group on its B ring.

RNA information: cTNT (cardiac troponin T) pre-mRNA is a splicing reporter RNA that contains an alternatively spliced exon 5. It is regulated by the RNA-binding protein MBNL1, which binds upstream of exon 5 and represses its inclusion. In myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), the toxic r(CUG)exp RNA sequesters MBNL1, leading to increased exon 5 inclusion in cTNT mRNA. Thus, cTNT splicing serves as a functional readout for MBNL1 activity and DM1-associated splicing defects in cellular models .
Small_molecule_information: N-[4-[6-(furan-2-carbonylamino)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenyl]furan-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Benzimidazole 16 (2015), is a complex aromatic heterocyclic compound centered around a benzimidazole core, a planar heterocycle formed by the fusion of a benzene and an imidazole ring, and Benzimidazole 16 (2015) is a 2-phenyl-benzimidazole-4-carboxamide derivative engineered to selectively bind the toxic r(CUG)109 RNA expansion that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1, the benzimidazole is substituted at position 2 with a 4-aminophenyl-furan-2-carboxamide side chain, and at position 6 with a furan-2-carbonylamino side chain, the entire molecule is composed of multiple planar aromatic rings (benzimidazole, phenyl, and two furan rings) linked by amide bonds, creating an extended, rigid, and highly conjugated system, the amide and benzimidazole moieties provide multiple hydrogen bond donors and acceptors, this scaffold is often designed as a protein kinase inhibitor or receptor ligand, benzimidazole compounds are a privileged scaffold in medicinal chemistry due to their diverse biological activities, frequently exhibiting antiparasitic, antiviral, and anticancer properties, the activity of this specific compound is likely related to its potential as a molecular probe or as a lead compound in the search for new drugs, by mimicking the recognition surface of MBNL1, it engages the periodic UU internal loops of the CUG hairpin with notable potency in TR-FRET displacement, displaces MBNL1 from ribonuclear foci, and restores correct alternative splicing of the cTNT mini-gene in DM1 cell models without altering DMPK mRNA abundance or affecting off-target RNAs such as human A-site rRNA or r(GGCC)4, its drug-like physicochemical profilemoderate molecular weight (352 Da), cLogP 3.9, topological polar surface area 111 , and negligible cytotoxicity up to 100 Mdemonstrates that uncharged, benzimidazole-based small molecules can selectively drug structured, repetitive RNA motifs and rescue disease-relevant splicing defects. RNA

RNA information: A10-RNA-Wt is a guanine (G)-rich sequence located in the 5' untranslated region (UTR) of ADAM10 mRNA, which can form a stable RNA G-quadruplex secondary structure. As a translational inhibitory element, this structure negatively regulates the protein synthesis of ADAM10 by adopting a specific spatial conformation that hinders ribosome binding or scanning. Studies have shown that targeting this structure with antisense oligonucleotides and small-molecule compounds (such as compound 24) can relieve its translational repression effect, up-regulate ADAM41 expression, thereby promoting the production of neuroprotective sAPP伪 and reducing the generation of peptides. This provides a novel strategy for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease.
Small_molecule_information: 9-ethyl-3-[2-(4-piperidin-1-ylquinolin-2-yl)ethenyl]carbazole, also referred to as Compound 32 (a green-yellow 1-methylquinolinium derivative), is a large aromatic molecule formed by linking a carbazole core and a quinoline unit via an ethenyl (alkene) bridge, with the quinoline being substituted at the 4-position with a 4-(2-methoxyethyl)piperidin-1-yl group and featuring a 2-(2-(9-ethyl-9H-carbazol-3-yl)vinyl) group at the quinoline 2-position; this structure defines a classic carbazole-quinoline fluorophore with an extended -conjugation system, exhibiting strong and often environmentally sensitive fluorescence, and in terms of its biological activity, SPR assay reveals its affinity for A10-RNA-Wt is lower than that of Compound 24, it was not subjected to the dual-luciferase reporter assay so its impact on ADAM10 translation remains unreported, and no clear cytotoxicity data (IC values) is provided for it in the tested compound tables, while in real applications, it is widely used as a fluorescent probe or dye for bioimaging and cell staining, primarily due to its ability to bind to DNA or RNA through a combination of stacking and electrostatic interactions, and it may also be investigated for antineoplastic activity via DNA intercalation or topoisomerase inhibition.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(E,2S)-1,8-bis[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-7-[(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-1,8-dioxooct-4-en-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 4E (the tetramethylated analog), is a complex N-methylated poly-cationic peptidomimetic based on the (E)-oct-4-en-2-yl scaffold, which stands out for its combination of high affinity, strong selectivity, and superior antiviral potency, binds HIV-1 FSS RNA with extremely strong affinity and demonstrates notable selectivity over off-target nucleic acids, and crucially shows the greatest membrane permeability of the series due to increased hydrophobicity and exhibits the strongest inhibition of HIV infectivity in pseudotyped assays, not only reducing the production of infectious virions but also impairing intracellular viral replication to become the most biologically effective compound reported, while the molecule has undergone N-methylation at multiple amide nitrogens within the peptide backbone and the terminal carbamoyl groups, and N-methylation is a critical medicinal chemistry strategy used to increase metabolic stability (resistance to peptidase degradation), reduce hydrogen bonding capacity, and enhance membrane permeability, aiming to improve oral bioavailability, and the molecule retains its poly-cationic and highly hydrophobic nature, serving as a candidate for development as a more orally bioavailable kinase inhibitor or anti-cancer agent.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-N-[(4-chlorophenyl)methyl]-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 11, is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with a 4-chlorobenzyl side chain modified from initial active analogs, which features the thieno[2,3-b]pyridine core, with the carboxamide at the 2-position linked to a 4-chlorobenzyl group; this 4-chlorobenzyl group is a common lipophilic substituent used to improve cell permeability and metabolic stability, and this N-benzyl substituted amide structure is frequently observed in kinase inhibitors, consequently, this molecule is a strong candidate for a kinase inhibitor intended for anti-tumor or anti-inflammatory research, and it also binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key elements for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs with moderate affinity while lacking further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity, and its structure represents a typical lead compound in medicinal chemistry used to probe the activity of the thienopyridine scaffold against specific drug targets.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[5-methyl-7-[2-(4-methylmorpholin-4-ium-4-yl)ethoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: The HIV-1 frameshift-stimulating stem-loop (FSS) RNA is a highly conserved structured element located within the gag-pol transcript that regulates production of the essential Gag-Pol polyprotein through a −1 ribosomal frameshift. It consists of a stable stem-loop positioned downstream of a slippery sequence that induces ribosomal pausing and promotes frameshifting in about 5–10% of translation events. This process allows translation to continue beyond the gag stop codon, producing Gag-Pol proteins such as protease, reverse transcriptase, and integrase that are required for infectious virion assembly. Altering FSS activity disrupts the Gag to Gag-Pol ratio, substantially reducing viral replication and infectivity.
Small_molecule_information: N-[(2S)-1-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S,4R)-2-[[(2S)-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]-4-hydroxypyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide is a complex, linear peptidic analog. Its structure features two (2S)-2-(N-methyl-(2S,4R)-4-hydroxy-2-(N-methyl-N-phenylalaninyl-3-aminopropanamide)pyrrolidine-1-carbonyl)butanoyl units linked by a 1,4-disubstituted 1,2,3-triazole ring, with both termini acylated by a 2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl group. Compared to N-[(2S)-1-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-4-[4-[(2S)-3-[(2S)-2-[[(2S)-1-amino-1-oxo-3-phenylpropan-2-yl]-methylcarbamoyl]pyrrolidin-1-yl]-2-[(2-ethylquinoline-3-carbonyl)-methylamino]-3-oxopropyl]triazol-1-yl]-1-oxobutan-2-yl]-2-ethyl-N-methylquinoline-3-carboxamide, a 4-hydroxy group (4R configuration) is introduced on the proline residue, and the terminal amino group of the phenylalanine is replaced by 3-aminopropylamine. The molecule contains multiple chiral centers and amide bonds. This compound adopts a non-planar, flexible conformation. The 4-hydroxy group enhances hydrophilicity and hydrogen bonding capability, and the quinoline core allows for aromatic stacking and hydrophobic interactions. In terms of biological function, peptides and their analogs generally have the potential to act as receptor ligands, enzyme inhibitors, or signaling molecules, and quinoline compounds are broadly known for diverse activities including antibacterial, antifungal, antimalarial, anthelmintic, spasmolytic, cardiotonic, anti-inflammatory, and analgesic effects.

RNA information: 5′AAC/3′U_G: This AAC/U_G motif represents an asymmetric loop that was enriched in binding assays but not highlighted as a major validated target for compounds with antiviral activity. Its structure provides potential recognition elements but no detailed functional data in the context of HCV was reported.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[3-[[2,5-bis[[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]carbamoyl]phenyl]carbamoylamino]phenyl]carbamoylamino]-1-N,4-N-bis[4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl]benzene-1,4-dicarboxamide, also referred to as Compound 7, is an extremely large, highly branched polycationic compound, and it can selectively bind internal loop motifs such as 5UUA/3A_U and 5UGU/3G_A with submicromolar affinity, with structural studies revealing that its binding is driven by recognition of asymmetric bulges within RNA stems, it can directly interact with HCV 3 UTR stemloops I and II by targeting motifs present in viral RNA, functional assays have demonstrated that it inhibits HCV replication in cell culture without affecting host ribosomal RNAs, and RNase H/ASO mapping has confirmed its intracellular engagement of the predicted binding sites; its intricate scaffold contains multiple phenyl rings, amide bonds, and urea groups, terminated by several 4-(4,5-dihydro-1H-imidazol-2-yl)phenyl substituents, structural analysis indicates that this molecule bears at least six imidazolinyl groups, making it a potent hexacationic system, this structural design is an extreme example used to construct supramolecular nucleic acid binders, where the molecular length, rigidity, and multiple charges enable high-affinity interactions with multiple sites on the DNA helix, including the minor groove, major groove, or phosphate backbone, and such compounds are often investigated as non-viral gene delivery vectors (by charge-neutralizing DNA), highly effective anticancer agents, or for use as DNA structural probes, aiming for extremely high binding stability and cytotoxicity.

RNA information: BS-xpt(U48G) is a guanine riboswitch mutant of Bacillus subtilis. Structurally, it alters the aptamer core’s local conformation, enhancing cleavage at positions 47/49 and reducing it at 48. Functionally, G48 introduction (context-independent) markedly impairs ligand (guanine/2AP) binding affinity but retains partial guanine-induced structural reorganization. Its in vivo transcriptional termination regulation efficiency declines compared to the wild-type BS-xpt.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-amino-9-bromo-2-(4-fluorobenzoyl)-4-phenylchromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one, also referred to as Compound 16, is a polycyclic heterocyclic ketone featuring a complex four-ring chromeno[4,3-b]pyridin-5-one core, this large, planar system is substituted at multiple positions: amino at 3, phenyl at 4, bromo at 9, and a 4-fluorobenzoyl group at 2, the molecules multiple aromatic rings and carbonyl groups contribute to its high planarity and conjugation, this structural motif is the basis for classic DNA intercalators or cell cycle inhibitors, capable of binding to nucleic acids or vital enzymes through stacking and hydrogen bonding, the bromo and fluoro atoms (which are the halogen substitutions of this compound) act as halogen substituents to enhance lipophilicity and metabolic stability, and it is worth noting that this compound displayed strong RNA-binding properties, was among the most potent compounds and reinforced the value of halogen atoms in improving drug-like properties, in practice, similar chromene derivatives are explored for their broad-spectrum anti-cancer activity or as potential photosensitizers for photodynamic therapy.

RNA information: G-quadruplex TERRA is a well-characterized, stable RNA G-quadruplex structure formed by the human telomeric repeat sequence. It serves as a canonical model for studying RNA G-quadruplex interactions. In this study, it was used as a control to demonstrate that although known G-quadruplex ligands bind TERRA with high affinity (low nM Kd), they are poor inhibitors of C9orf72 RAN translation. This key finding confirms that the toxic pathology in c9ALS/FTD is driven primarily by the hairpin form of r(G4C2)exp rather than the G-quadruplex form, highlighting the therapeutic importance of targeting the specific hairpin structure.
Small_molecule_information: tetrakis(4-methylbenzenesulfonic acid);5,10,15,20-tetrakis(1-methylpyridin-1-ium-4-yl)-21,23-dihydroporphyrin, also referred to as TMPyP4, is an organic salt containing a porphyrin macrocycle and toluenesulfonate counterions, which is a cationic porphyrin and a widely used pan-G-quadruplex binder that bound the r(G4C2) quadruplex with high affinity among the tested ligands and whose inability to inhibit RAN translation was particularly significant, proving that even high affinity for the quadruplex does not translate to functional activity against the pathogenic hairpin; its structure is based on the porphyrin parent skeleton: four pyrrole rings linked by methine bridges form a conjugated macrocycle, where the four meso-positions of the macrocycle are substituted with 1-methylpyridinium-4-yl groups, and in terms of biological function, porphyrin compounds, due to their unique structure and photosensitivity, are often used in photodynamic therapy, bioimaging probes, and as DNA-binding agents (e.g., TMPyP).

RNA information: The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: 2-[[11-[3-(dimethylamino)propylamino]-5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-7-yl]oxy]ethyl-trimethylazanium diiodide, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine and quaternary ammonium salt side chains. The presence of the quaternary ammonium salt typically confers higher polarity and DNA binding affinity, making it a potential potent anti-tumor agent or a fluorescent probe in biological research.

RNA information: HIV-1 Ψ SL3 is a key element of the viral Ψ packaging domain, featuring a G9-G10-A11-G12 tetranucleotide loop and a stem structure. Its core function is the specific binding to the Gag polyprotein (especially the nucleocapsid domain), which is a critical step for the packaging of the viral genomic RNA into virions. Additionally, this element is highly conserved and serves as a potential target for antiviral therapy.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[(1-methyl-6-nitroquinolin-4-ylidene)amino]-N-[4-[(1-methyl-4-pyridinylidene)amino]phenyl]benzamide, also referred to as NSC260594 (a quinolinium derivative structurally similar to known DNA-binding compounds), is a complex quinoline-pyridine-benzamide derivative; this compound not only is typically designed to explore anti-tumor activity due to their potential as DNA intercalators and topoisomerase inhibitors but also effectively inhibits HIV-1 replication by specifically binding to the G9-G10-A11-G12 tetraloop region within the viral RNA packaging signal SL3, thereby obstructing the interaction between the Gag protein and the RNA and consequently suppressing viral particle packaging and release, with its mechanism of action confirmed by H NMR spectroscopy involving a specific binding interaction that is distinctly different from the mode of action of the non-specific intercalator ellipticine.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol, also referred to as Amodiaquine Di HCl, is a quinoline derivative structured around a quinoline core, with a chloro substitution at the 7-position and an amino link at the 4-position to a benzene ring containing a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group (molecular formula C20H22ClN3O), and it is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory effects that shares mechanisms with chloroquine but has higher toxicity risks, limiting its use despite efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains; this compound possesses a planar conjugated system and multiple functional groups (chloro, amine, phenol, quinoline), functionally, the quinoline core is famous as the scaffold for well-known antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine), and derivatives are commonly investigated for broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antimalarial, and anti-cancer activities, the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups suggests potential for metal complexation or binding to enzyme active sites.

RNA information: LM-pbuG is a guanine riboswitch from Listeria monocytogenes. Its structural feature consists of a highly conserved three-stem ring region, which is organized around a three-directional junction point to form the guanine binding site. The function of LM-pbuG is to specifically recognize and bind to guanine through its aptamer region, thereby regulating gene expression. When guanine binds to the aptamer region, it causes a rearrangement of RNA structure. This structural change can lead to the termination or continuation of transcription, thereby controlling the expression of downstream genes. In the experiment, LM-pbuG demonstrated a binding affinity for 2-aminopine (2AP), indicating that it also has a high affinity for guanine. This difference in binding affinity may reflect the response ability of different ribose switches to changes in ligand concentration in vivo, thereby playing a key role in the metabolic regulation and gene expression of bacteria. This regulatory mechanism of LM-pbuG enables it to respond rapidly when the intracellular guanine concentration changes, thereby precisely regulating the expression levels of related genes.
Small_molecule_information: 7H-purin-2-amine, also referred to as 2-aminopine2AP, is a fluorescently labeled purine analogue and a fluorescent derivative of adenine with the 2-position hydrogen replaced by an amino group, which is a nitrogen-heterocyclic aromatic compound, often used to study the ligand binding characteristics of RNA molecules, especially riboswitches. The structure of 2AP is similar to that of adenine and guanine, and it can be embedded in the pbuE ribose switch like adenine, which makes it suitable as a substitute for these bases to study the interaction between RNA and ligands and can be used as a probe to reveal the dynamic-thermodynamic control mechanism of the ribose switch. Functionally, 2AP is used as a probe to detect and quantify the binding affinity of ribose switches for purine ligands. When 2AP binds to the aptamer region of the ribose switch, it causes a significant weakening of 2AP fluorescence. This is because 2AP is completely surrounded by RNA in the ligand binding pocket of the ribose switch. By monitoring the changes in 2AP fluorescence, researchers can infer the affinity of the ribose switch for ligand binding, as well as the changes in the ribose switch structure before and after ligand binding.

Small_molecule_information: 3,5-diamino-6-chloro-N-(diaminomethylidene)pyrazine-2-carboxamide, also referred to as Amiloride, is a well-known potassium-sparing diuretic that blocks epithelial sodium channels (ENaC) in the kidneys, with the molecule acting by reversibly blocking the epithelial sodium channel (ENaC) in the renal collecting ducts, thereby reducing sodium reabsorption and fluid retention. Clinically, Amiloride is used to treat hypertension, congestive heart failure, and edema, and is often combined with other diuretics to prevent hypokalemia.

RNA information: It forms a stable RG4 structure, contributing to translational inhibition.
Small_molecule_information: 4,11-bis(2-aminoethylamino)naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione, also referred to as a thiophene-fused analogue of 2a with similar side chains, is an organic compound featuring a complex polycyclic core of naphthoquinone and benzothiole, forming the naphtho[2,3-f][1]benzothiole-5,10-dione skeleton. This framework is a sulfur-containing heteroaromatic system with a highly conjugated electron structure and redox-active quinone carbonyl groups at positions 5 and 10. The substituents at positions 4 and 11 are two 2-aminoethylamino groups, which introduce primary amines capable of protonation, granting the molecule polycationic character and increasing its water solubility under physiological conditions. This specific structural combination, characterized by its planarity, extended conjugation, redox activity, and cationic side chains, makes it a molecule of interest in biomedicine, typically investigated as a potential DNA intercalator or topoisomerase inhibitor, suggesting potential cytotoxic or anticancer activities; it has moderate cellular uptake, stabilizes RG4s, and also suppresses translation and promotes apoptosis, though less potently than 2a.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(2-aminophenyl)-N-[4-[2-(diethylamino)ethoxy]phenyl]quinazolin-4-amine, also referred to as a11 and a12, is a quinazoline derivative that shares structural similarity with the pharmacophore of many Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKIs), such as Gefitinib; while a11 and a12 are only confirmed to bind G-quadruplex sequences in vitro, with their screening target hVEGF-A closely associated with tumor angiogenesis yet merely serving as members of the screening library for potential anti-tumor candidates without further functional or disease-related validation, this compound is often developed as a potential anticancer agent targeting the EGFR or other kinases in cellular signaling pathways.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (2R,3R,4R,5R)-4-amino-2-[(2S,3R,4S,6R)-4,6-diamino-3-[(1R,2S)-2-amino-6-(aminomethyl)cyclohexyl]oxy-2-hydroxycyclohexyl]oxy-5-methyloxane-3,5-diol, also referred to as Sisomicin Sulphate, is a complex aminoglycoside compound featuring multiple sugar rings and an aminocyclohexitol structure linked by glycosidic bonds. The molecule contains numerous amine (NH2) groups, rendering it polycationic at physiological pH. Aminoglycosides are a critical class of broad-spectrum antibiotics that function by irreversibly binding to the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, disrupting protein synthesis by binding to rRNA, inhibiting protein synthesis. Similar to clinical antibiotics like Streptomycin or Gentamicin, this molecule is primarily used to treat severe Gram-negative bacterial infections, though its therapeutic use must consider inherent ototoxicity and nephrotoxicity side effects.

RNA information: HIV-1 RRE RNA is a highly structured regulatory element of HIV-1, shaped by specific base-pairing and loop regions. Its conserved binding pocket, with key residues like G70 and specific C residues, specifically binds the viral Rev protein, facilitating nuclear export of unspliced viral mRNAs—critical for HIV replication. It is a promising antiviral target, as synthetic ligands (e.g., thienopyridine derivatives) block the Rev-RRE interaction to inhibit viral propagation.
Small_molecule_information: (3-amino-6-methylthieno[2,3-b]pyridin-2-yl)-morpholin-4-ylmethanone, also referred to as Compound 14, is a thienopyridine carboxamide derivative with a morphinyl side chain modified from initial active analogs, containing thienopyridine and morpholine heterocyclic rings. Its structure is based on the thienopyridine parent skeleton: a thiophene ring is fused with a pyridine ring, substituted with an amino group at C3, a methyl group at C6, and a morpholinylcarbonyl group at C2. In terms of biological function, thienopyridine compounds are known for their diverse pharmacological activities, including anticancer, antiviral, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial effects, and this compound binds HIV-1 TAR RNA and RRE RNA key for viral replication, targeting HIV-1 infection by interacting with these regulatory RNAs, showing moderate affinity and no further functional validation for anti-HIV-1 activity.

RNA information: RNA1 is a structured RNA oligonucleotide derived from the SARS-CoV-2 5′UTR SL5 region, engineered to contain a single G bulge. It adopts a hairpin structure and serves as a model RNA for studying selective small-molecule binding, especially with coumarin derivatives, using fluorescence polarization and MST assays.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-4H-thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazine, also referred to as SMSM6, is a coumarin-derived small molecule characterized by the 4H-thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazine core, a fused tricyclic system containing sulfur and nitrogen atoms, linked to an 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridine ring and a 4-methylpiperazine group, which functions as a selective RNA binder targeting single G bulges with high selectivity over other bulge types and GA-rich loops, and its rod-like, planar structure and positive charge facilitate minor groove binding, supported by computational and experimental data, positioning it as a key compound for studying RNA-ligand interactions, while thiazine derivatives have a long history in medicinal chemistry, with many anti-inflammatory, anti-allergic, and diuretic drugs containing this structure, the molecule has an 8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl group at C2 and a 4-methylpiperazin-1-yl group at C6, importantly, the piperazine ring at C6 is not quaternized, making the compound a neutral or weak base at physiological pH, this lack of a fixed positive charge results in higher lipophilicity, which facilitates membrane penetration and intracellular action, therefore, 2-(8-fluoro-2-methylimidazo[1,2-a]pyridin-6-yl)-6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-4H-thieno[2,3-d][1,3]thiazine is likely designed to target intracellular enzymes or GPCRs, such as a potential PDE inhibitor or histamine receptor antagonist.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-[(7-chloroquinolin-4-yl)amino]-6-(diethylaminomethyl)phenol, also referred to as Amodiaquine Di HCl, is a quinoline derivative structured around a quinoline core, with a chloro substitution at the 7-position and an amino link at the 4-position to a benzene ring containing a phenolic hydroxyl group and a diethylaminomethyl group (molecular formula C20H22ClN3O), and it is an antimalarial drug with immunomodulatory effects that shares mechanisms with chloroquine but has higher toxicity risks, limiting its use despite efficacy against resistant Plasmodium strains; this compound possesses a planar conjugated system and multiple functional groups (chloro, amine, phenol, quinoline), functionally, the quinoline core is famous as the scaffold for well-known antimalarial drugs (such as chloroquine), and derivatives are commonly investigated for broad-spectrum antimicrobial, antimalarial, and anti-cancer activities, the presence of the phenolic hydroxyl and amine groups suggests potential for metal complexation or binding to enzyme active sites.

RNA information: 5'CUG/3'CUG is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it folds into an A-form hairpin with characteristic UU mismatches, stabilized by base-pairing. Functionally, it sequesters Muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, disrupting pre-mRNA alternative splicing, which is the core gain-of-function mechanism underlying DM1 pathogenesis.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Nitidine, is another methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid, similar to 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium but with different methoxy substitution (2,3-positions), and as a benzophenanthridine alkaloid it also possesses antitumor and antimalarial activity, can inhibit topoisomerases and telomerase to disrupt DNA replication, and its fluorescent properties aid in cellular imaging studies; due to its planar, rigid, and positively charged structural features, its primary activity in practice is as a DNA intercalator and cytotoxic agent, exhibiting strong anti-tumor properties, minor changes in the substitution pattern (from 1,2- to 2,3-dimethoxy) are critical in drug design to modulate the molecules charge distribution and steric bulk, which can alter its interaction strength and selectivity for specific DNA sequences or Topoisomerase isozymes, and this makes it valuable in the structure-activity relationship studies of novel anticancer agents.

RNA information: TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-1 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-2 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]-2-(cyclohexylmethoxy)phenyl]ethanamine, also referred to as Compound 5d (a 3,5,2,6-tetrakis(2-aminoethyl) terphenyl compound with a single methoxy group at the 2 position of the central benzene ring), is a derivative of 2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-[4-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]phenol, where the phenol is substituted with a cyclohexylmethoxy group, the compound was synthesized via sequential palladium-catalyzed Suzuki couplings and characterized by methods like NMR and SPR, the cyclohexyl group is a saturated alicyclic ring that can significantly increase the molecules lipophilicity but removes the possibility of - stacking, as a tetracationic polyamine, this increase in lipophilicity may enhance its interaction with lipid-rich environments (such as the interior of cell membranes), functionally, Compound 5d binds to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB with low binding affinity and has corresponding binding specificity relative to control hairpins GGh and TARh, shows higher IC in inhibiting the Bh-Rev interaction and no antiviral activity in cell assays, while such derivatives may be explored for targeting membrane-associated proteins or interfering with lipid metabolism pathways, for instance, as novel antiviral or antifungal agents.

RNA information: (5'CAG/3'GUC): This RNA motif represents a standard Watson-Crick base-paired duplex with CAG pairing with GUC. It serves as a control structure to compare against mismatched or disease-relevant RNA motifs. It exhibits typical A-form helical geometry and is used to assess the selectivity of small molecules for non-canonical structures.; 5'CAG/3'GUC is a classic paired RNA sequence used as a control.; In contrast, (5'CAG/3'GUC) forms a standard, stable duplex with canonical Watson-Crick base pairing (A-U and G-C). The absence of mismatches makes it a structurally bland control, crucial for demonstrating the binding specificity of compounds towards the aberrant A-A motif.
Small_molecule_information: 3-(4-fluorophenyl)-2,5-dimethyl-7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidin-1-ium-4-imine, also referred to as CP1 which features an imidazopyrimidine fused heterocyclic core with multiple nitrogen atoms, substituted by two methyl groups and a 4-fluorophenyl ring, is based on the 7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidine core, which is an important purine nucleobase analog (7-deazaguanine); the molecule has an unusual electronic structure, being quaternized at N1 and featuring an imine (CN) at C4, forming a 1-ium-4-imine system, and it is substituted with a 4-fluorophenyl group at C3 and methyl groups at C2 and C5, while its binding data showed higher affinity for the control RNA, indicating a lack of specificity for the pathogenic AA mismatch motif, which limited its further development as a lead compound, and this unique ionic and imine-containing nucleobase analog makes 3-(4-fluorophenyl)-2,5-dimethyl-7H-pyrrolo[2,3-d]pyrimidin-1-ium-4-imine an excellent candidate for antimetabolites or enzyme inhibitors that interfere with nucleic acid biosynthesis or metabolism, and it may be designed as a novel antiviral or anti-cancer agent by mimicking natural substrates.

RNA information: NA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Its core structural feature is the simultaneous inclusion of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515 and the adjacent Compound 1-binding motif, and the combination of these two motifs provides conditions for dual-site binding.; RNA 4 (Drosha and adjacent 515) is an RNA fragment mimicking the Drosha processing site and its flanking region in pri-miR-515. Its core structural feature is the simultaneous inclusion of the Drosha processing site motif unique to pri-miR-515 and the adjacent Compound 1-binding motif, and the combination of these two motifs provides conditions for dual-site binding.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[4-[[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[[2-[(2-amino-2-oxoethyl)-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]-propylamino]-2-oxoethyl]amino]methyl]triazol-1-yl]propyl]-4-[2,6-ditert-butyl-4-[5-[6-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]-1H-benzimidazol-2-yl]phenoxy]butanamide, also referred to as Compound 1a (a peptoid derivative of Compound 1 formed by attaching Compound 1 to a peptoid linker that serves as a control to evaluate the impact of the peptoid scaffold on binding affinity), is a huge, modular molecule combining a well-known bis-benzimidazole DNA-binding unit with a 2,6-di-tert-butylphenol moiety, linked via a triazole-propylamine spacer to a long, flexible poly(glycinamide)-like chain; binding assays have shown that it binds to RNA 4 and compared with Compound 1, it only exerts a modest effect on the binding affinity, which confirms that the peptoid scaffold does not contribute significantly to RNA binding, and its structure suggests a dual mechanism or application: the bis-benzimidazole part confers potent nucleic acid binding capability, suitable for development as an anticancer agent, while the di-tert-butylphenol moiety is a recognized antioxidant or free radical scavenger pharmacophore, and in reality, such complex conjugates are explored as chemical tools or advanced drug delivery systems to achieve high affinity binding to biological macromolecules, possibly serving as gene delivery vectors or novel therapeutics.

RNA information: 5'CNG/3'CNG refers to repetitive RNA sequences (N = any nucleotide), commonly located in 5'/3' UTRs. Structurally, it forms stable hairpin or G-quadruplex structures. Functionally, it regulates post-transcriptional gene expression. Pathogenically, expanded subtypes (e.g., CUG/CAG repeats) cause microsatellite expansion disorders like myotonic dystrophy type 1, sequestering RNA-binding proteins (e.g., MBNL1) to disrupt alternative splicing.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(4-carbamimidoylphenyl)-1H-indole-6-carboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 20 (DAPI, 4,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), is a compound containing an indole scaffold and bis-imidamide groups, one imidamide directly attached to the indole ring and the other attached via a phenyl ring at the C2 position of indole, with the formula C16H14N4, and it is also a commercially available guanidino/amidino-containing compound, a routine nuclear stain and chemical progenitor of derivative D6; this indole diamidine combines the lipophilicity of indole with the cationic nature of the diamidine groups, and it can bind influenza A RNA promoter (causes NMR perturbations), AT-rich dsDNA minor groove, and r(CAG)exps 5CAG/3GAC loop, disrupt r(CAG)expMBN1, correct splicing defects in Huntingtons/ataxia type 3 models though it has cytotoxicity that limits direct optimization, this combination also results in an effective DNA minor groove binder with potent antimicrobial and antitumor activity, and it is researched for developing new anticancer agents with high target specificity and enhanced pharmacokinetic profiles.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: (7S,9S)-7-[(2R,4S,5R,6S)-4-amino-5-hydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxy-6,9,11-trihydroxy-9-(2-hydroxyacetyl)-4-methoxy-8,10-dihydro-7H-tetracene-5,12-dione, also referred to as Epirubicin, is the chemical name for, or a very close analogue of, the anthracycline antibiotic Epirubicin, which is an anthracycline chemotherapeutic agent that intercalates into DNA, inhibiting topoisomerase II and causing DNA strand breaks; Epirubicin is a widely used chemotherapy drug in clinical practice for treating various cancers such as breast cancer and ovarian cancer, and its mechanism involves DNA intercalation and Topoisomerase II inhibition.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 3-[(17-methoxy-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-yl)oxy]propan-1-amine This molecule features a 3-aminopropoxy chain connected via an ether linkage. This structure, containing a primary amine, also functions as a dicationic species (quaternary core plus protonated primary amine) under physiological conditions. Compounds with the propylamine backbone are often studied as Histamine H2 receptor antagonists or antidepressants. Its structure gives it the potential for strong electrostatic interactions with negatively charged cellular components (like DNA) or the phospholipid membrane, which may confer antimicrobial activity or utility as a gene transfection aid.

RNA information: HIV-1 TAR RNA is a conserved stem-bulge-loop hairpin at the 5′ end of all HIV-1 transcripts, existing as 29–59-nt fragments. Its structure features a double-stranded stem, a 3-base pyrimidine bulge (e.g., UCU), and a 6-nt loop. Functionally, it binds Tat’s arginine-rich segment, recruits P-TEFb, boosts RNA Pol II processivity (viral gene expression >100-fold), aids reverse transcription, and is a key therapeutic target for HIV infections.
Small_molecule_information: N-[5-(5,7-dimethoxy-1-methyl-4-oxoquinolin-2-yl)-2-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropoxy)phenyl]-4-(dimethylamino)butanamide, also referred to as Phenylquinolone 3b, is the product of substituting the primary amine on the phenyl ring of 2-[3-amino-4-(3-piperidin-1-ylpropoxy)phenyl]-5,7-dimethoxy-1-methylquinolin-4-one with 4-(dimethylamino)butanamide, and Phenylquinolone 3b is a disubstituted derivative, it is synthesized from the monosubstituted analog 2b by further acylation with 4-(dimethylamino)butyric acid (DMABA), which introduces a second protonable cationic side chain, this makes the molecule a highly complex and basic multifunctional molecule, combining amide, piperidine tertiary amine, and tertiary amine functional groups, this structure is often designed as a multi-target drug, intended to simultaneously inhibit multiple signaling pathways in cancer, such as kinases and proteases, in practical applications, its complexity aims to provide greater potency, better selectivity, and the ability to overcome drug resistance issues with existing therapies, this structural feature was found to be unfavorable, as the compound exhibited reduced in vitro activity in inhibiting the Tat/TAR complex compared to its monosubstituted precursor, its activity profile aligns with the observed trend that the presence of a second basic side chain is detrimental to effective target binding, supporting the conclusion that only one cationic substituent is optimal for activity in this series.

RNA information: 5'CGG/3'GGC is a pathogenic RNA motif linked to fragile X-associated tremor/ataxia syndrome (FXTAS), a trinucleotide repeat expansion disorder (TRED). Structurally, it forms a hairpin with a 1×1 internal loop, closed by CG pairs, adopting non-canonical folding. Functionally, it contributes to FXTAS pathogenesis, likely by sequestering cellular proteins and disrupting normal splicing. As a disease-relevant RNA, it serves as a target for screening therapeutic small molecules.
Small_molecule_information: 2,3-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium, also referred to as Nitidine, is another methylated benzophenanthridine quaternary alkaloid, similar to 1,2-dimethoxy-12-methyl-[1,3]benzodioxolo[5,6-c]phenanthridin-12-ium but with different methoxy substitution (2,3-positions), and as a benzophenanthridine alkaloid it also possesses antitumor and antimalarial activity, can inhibit topoisomerases and telomerase to disrupt DNA replication, and its fluorescent properties aid in cellular imaging studies; due to its planar, rigid, and positively charged structural features, its primary activity in practice is as a DNA intercalator and cytotoxic agent, exhibiting strong anti-tumor properties, minor changes in the substitution pattern (from 1,2- to 2,3-dimethoxy) are critical in drug design to modulate the molecules charge distribution and steric bulk, which can alter its interaction strength and selectivity for specific DNA sequences or Topoisomerase isozymes, and this makes it valuable in the structure-activity relationship studies of novel anticancer agents.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: 17-methoxy-N-(2-piperidin-1-ylethyl)-5,7-dioxa-13-azoniapentacyclo[11.8.0.0{2,10}.0{4,8}.0{15,20}]henicosa-1(13),2,4(8),9,14,16,18,20-octaen-16-amine chloride This molecule possesses a 2-piperidin-1-ylethyl side chain, where the piperidine ring is linked via a two-carbon ethyl spacer to the quaternary core. The piperidine-ethylamine scaffold is the foundation for many local anesthetics and antiarrhythmic drugs (similar to structures like Lidocaine). In real-world pharmacology, this compound is highly likely to be studied for voltage-gated sodium channel blockade, which stabilizes the membrane potential in neural or myocardial cells. The shorter ethyl spacer brings the piperidine ring closer to the rigid core, which may influence the depth of binding and conformation at the channel receptor site.

RNA information: The influenza A virus RNA promoter features a bent A-form helical structure with a dynamic (A–A)–U internal loop that serves as a specific binding site for the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) and nucleoprotein complex. This unique structural motif exhibits conformational flexibility that facilitates recognition by viral replication machinery while maintaining overall A-form geometry. The promoter's distinctive architecture and dynamic properties are essential for initiating viral RNA synthesis, making it a critical functional element in the influenza replication cycle and a promising target for antiviral therapeutic development; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Its structure, determined by NMR spectroscopy, adopts a panhandle-like conformation characterized by an A-form helix. A key structural feature is a unique (A-A)-U internal loop and a C-A mismatch pair. Furthermore, the promoter exhibits a significant bend of approximately 46 ± 10° near the viral RNA initiation site. Functionally, this specific architecture is specifically recognized by the viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp), which binds to it to initiate both transcription and replication of the viral genome. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. The high conservation of its sequence across strains and its intolerance to mutations, which are detrimental to viral replication, establish the influenza RNA promoter as a novel and promising druggable target for antiviral development.; the influenza A virus RNA promoter is a highly conserved structural element located at the 3' and 5' ends of each viral RNA segment. Small_molecule_information: 2-[2-(5-carbamimidoyl-1-benzofuran-2-yl)ethyl]-1-benzofuran-5-carboximidamide is a bis-amidine-benzofuran derivative, with two amidine groups attached to two benzofuran rings. The benzofuran core is often found in molecules with antineoplastic, antioxidant, and antimicrobial activities. This bis-amidine structure might be an effective DNA minor groove binder, studied for antiparasitic diseases (e.g., leishmaniasis) or antiviral therapy.

RNA information: 5'CAG/3'CAG is a pathogenic RNA model linked to polyglutamine diseases like Huntington’s disease (HD) and spinocerebellar ataxias (SCAs). Structurally, it forms a stable hairpin with non-canonical A-A mismatches, creating unique structural pockets. Functionally, it mimics expanded CAG repeat-associated structural aberrations and serves as a high-affinity target for small molecules, representing key pathogenic RNA features in related neurodegenerative disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)-5,7-dihydroxy-3-[(2S,3R,4S,5S,6R)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-[[(2R,3R,4R,5R,6S)-3,4,5-trihydroxy-6-methyloxan-2-yl]oxymethyl]oxan-2-yl]oxychromen-4-one, also referred to as Rutin, is a naturally occurring flavonoid glycoside found in plants, known for its antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and vasoprotective properties; Rutin is widely used as a dietary supplement and medicinal agent due to its potent antioxidant and free-radical scavenging activities, it stabilizes blood vessels, reduces capillary fragility, may protect against oxidative stress-related diseases, clinically it is known for its Vitamin P activity, primarily used to strengthen capillary walls, reduce capillary permeability, and treat conditions like varicose veins, hemorrhoids, and capillary fragility, it also exhibits anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties, and its poor water solubility limits bioavailability, but derivatives are explored for enhanced delivery.

RNA information: The IRES-A (MU1) is a mutant with a G-tract mutation. While it still has the potential to form a G-quadruplex, its structural stability is lower compared to the wild-type sequence.
Small_molecule_information: N,N-dimethyl-N-[7-[(1-methylpiperidin-4-yl)methoxy]-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl]propane-1,3-diamine, this compound is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with long diamine or quaternary ammonium salt side chains. This structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents. Real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: Bacillus subtilis 108yjdF RNA is a ribose switch with a unique structure and function, composed of 108 nucleotides, forming a four-stem ring structure, including four stem ring structures P1, P2, P3 and P4. Among them, the P1 and P2 stem-ring structures play a key role in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA can bind to a variety of azaaromatics, such as proflavine, chelerythrine and staurosporine. These compounds induce almost identical RNA structural changes when binding, indicating that their binding pockets have a high degree of universality in recognizing these compounds. Specifically, disrupting the base pairing of P1 (M1 mutant) will significantly reduce the binding affinity of propanin, while restoring the base pairing of P1 (M2 mutant) will bring the binding affinity back to a level close to that of the wild type. Furthermore, altering the conserved A or G nucleotides in the P2 loop (M3 and M4 mutants) completely eliminates the binding of propanin and riboflavin, further confirming the significance of these conserved nucleotides in ligand binding. 108yjdF RNA regulates gene expression by controlling the accessibility of ribosomal binding sites (RBS). When ligands bind, the RNA structure changes, making RBS accessible and thereby activating gene expression. Although the natural ligand of 108yjdF RNA has not yet been determined, the universality of its binding pocket and the mechanism of gene expression regulation provide important clues for studying its physiological functions within cells. It is speculated that it may have evolved into a universal sensor capable of recognizing multiple natural azaaromatics. These compounds may be toxic to cells, and the YjdF protein may be involved in the detoxification process.
Small_molecule_information: 4-aminobenzoic acid is commonly known as p-Aminobenzoic acid (PABA), an amino acid and carboxylic acid derivative, structured as a benzoic acid molecule with an amino group at position 4 of the benzene ring, molecular formula C7H7NO2, and 4-Aminobenzoic acid is an aromatic amino acid characterized by an amino group and a carboxyl group attached to a benzene ring, in terms of real-world activity, PABA plays a crucial role as a biochemical precursor in microorganisms and plants, it is an essential intermediate required by bacteria for the synthesis of folic acid, sulfonamide drugs (e.g., sulfamethoxazole) exert their potent antibacterial effect by structurally mimicking PABA and competitively inhibiting the key enzyme (dihydropteroate synthase) in the bacterial folic acid synthesis pathway, for humans, PABA is a non-essential nutrient, but its derivatives were once widely used in sunscreens for their effective UVB absorption properties, the figure indicates that 4-aminobenzoic acid has weak binding affinity and does not induce gene expression, suggesting that it may not be an active ligand for 108yjdF RNA.

Small_molecule_information: 7-methoxy-1-methyl-4,9-dihydro-3H-pyrido[3,4-b]indole, also referred to as harmaline, is a tetrahydro--carboline (THBC) derivative which is a planar azaaromatic -carboline alkaloid with a fused tricyclic core (indole and pyridine rings, containing multiple nitrogen atoms) and a methoxy group at the 7-position, plus a hydrogenated double bond in the pyridine ring (distinct from harmine), and THBCs are often involved in neurotransmitter modulation in the brain, with this compound binding to the yjdF motif RNA (azaaromatic riboswitch) via non-specific interactions but with lower affinity than chelerythrine, failing to activate riboswitch-mediated gene expression in Bacillus subtilis while having monoamine oxidase inhibitory activity which aids in studying riboswitch ligand structure-activity relationships, and THBCs are explored for their neuroprotective and antioxidant properties, with this molecule being a candidate for research as a ligand for 5-HT (serotonin) or dopamine receptors in the context of neurological and psychiatric drug development.

RNA information: The NRQ is a 18-nucleotide RNA oligomer corresponding to the wild-type, guanine-rich sequence (5′-GGGAGGGGCGGGUCUGGG-3′) located in the 5′-UTR of the human NRAS proto-oncogene mRNA. This sequence possesses the intrinsic ability to fold into a stable, parallel RNA G-quadruplex structure, which has been previously established to act as a translational repressor by inhibiting the initiation of NRAS protein synthesis. In this study, it serves as the primary target structure for the novel MSQ derivatives. Its confirmed G-quadruplex formation, characterized by a specific CD spectrum with a positive peak at 262 nm that is enhanced in the presence of potassium ions, and its functional role make it essential for evaluating the binding affinity and specificity of potential ligands designed to stabilize this structure and further repress translation.
Small_molecule_information: N-([1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, also referred to as the previously synthesized quindoline derivative that served as the control compound in the study, is a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-11-yl)-N,N-dimethylpropane-1,3-diamine, a benzofuro[3,2-b]quinoline derivative with a diamine side chain; this control compound once exhibited very strong binding affinity for the NRAS RNA G-quadruplex (NRQ) but demonstrated a profound lack of selectivity as it also bound significantly to the NRQ mutant which cannot form a canonical G-quadruplex structure, and this non-selective binding profile, likely due to strong, non-specific electrostatic interactions from its cationic nature, underscores the major drawback of previous-generation G-quadruplex ligands, while this structural class is known for DNA/RNA binding and topoisomerase inhibition, with many such compounds developed as broad-spectrum anticancer agents and its real-world applications may involve their use as novel anti-tumor leads or fluorescent probes for cancer therapy or biological research.

RNA information: r(CUG)2 is an RNA motif found in the expanded CUG repeat RNA (r(CUG)exp) that causes myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). It forms a stable stem-loop structure with two CUG repeats and can specifically bind to muscleblind-like 1 (MBNL1) protein, leading to its sequestration and the resulting misregulation of alternative splicing. This RNA-protein interaction contributes to the molecular pathology of DM1, and small molecules targeting r(CUG)2 can disrupt the complex and restore splicing defects in disease models.
Small_molecule_information: (E,2S,7S)-N,N-bis[(2S)-6-amino-1-(3-aminopropylamino)-1-oxohexan-2-yl]-2,7-bis[[(2S)-1-(2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl)pyrrolidine-2-carbonyl]amino]oct-4-enediamide, also referred to as DCC 5 (2012), is the E-isomer counterpart of compound 4, differing only in the configuration of the olefin bridge, and this geometric change alters the positioning of the benzo[g]quinoline group relative to the peptide scaffold, leading to slightly different recognition properties, while it retains overall high binding capability, though its cooperativity and selectivity are reduced compared to the Z-isomer, and structurally, it highlights how subtle stereochemical differences strongly affect RNA recognition, and biologically, it is still active in cellular models and displaces pathogenic complexes, demonstrating that both isomers contribute valuable insights into structureactivity relationships for RNA-binding molecules; it is a highly complex, multi-cationic peptidomimetic structure, its core is the (E)-oct-4-enediamide scaffold, with side chains derived from proline, 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline-3-carbonyl, and lysine, the molecule contains multiple primary amino groups, rendering it poly-cationic at physiological pH, the 2-ethylbenzo[g]quinoline is a bulky, highly hydrophobic aromatic group, this combination of poly-cationic character, multiple chiral centers, and large hydrophobic groups makes it suitable as an anti-tumor agent, a DNA/RNA binding agent, or a novel antibiotic, and the (E)-alkene provides a specific, rigid conformation.

RNA information: (CUG)12 is an RNA model of expanded pathogenic CUG repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 (DM1). Structurally, it forms a stable A-form hairpin with UU mismatches in its double-stranded region, stabilized by base-pairing and phosphate backbone interactions. Functionally, it high-affinity sequesters MBNL1 protein, disrupting alternative splicing, causing ribonuclear foci accumulation and pre-mRNA mis-splicing (e.g., insulin receptor exon 11 exclusion), via a toxic gain-of-function mechanism, making it a DM1 therapeutic target.
Small_molecule_information: N-[3-[3-(3-acetamidopropylamino)propylamino]propyl]-9-[4-[(4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazin-2-yl)amino]butylamino]acridine-4-carboxamide, also referred to as Compound 1 (Acridine 1), is a complex compound based on the acridine-4-carboxamide scaffold, with the tricyclic aromatic acridine core that in this molecule is rationally employed to selectively bind toxic (CUG)\ RNA repeats in myotonic dystrophy type 1 through stacking and hydrogen bonding, while its multiple amino and amide groups, long-chain alkylamine linkers, and polyamine-containing side chains enhance solubility, permeability, and nuclear entry, and the appended 4,6-diamino-1,3,5-triazine group at position 9 together with the extended N-linked polyamine chain contribute to potent nucleic-acid intercalation and disruption of pathogenic RNAprotein interactions, suggesting potential bioactivity including therapeutic relevance in DM1 as well as possible roles in broader anticancer or antibacterial contexts.

Small_molecule_information: 2-acetyloxybenzoic acid, also referred to as acetylsalicylic acid (Aspirin), is an aromatic carboxylate ester compound, with benzoic acid as its parent structure: the hydroxyl group at position 2 of benzoic acid is substituted by an acetyl group to form an ester bond, and this compound, with the molecular formula C9H8O4, is the chemical name for the well-known non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug Aspirin, which exhibits antipyretic, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antiplatelet aggregation activities, while its structural features result in weak binding to the 108yjdF RNA and inability to induce riboswitch-mediated gene expression, indicating it is not an active ligand for this RNA.

RNA information: G4-EBNA1 is a stable G-quadruplex in the EBNA1 mRNA GAr region. It binds nucleolin (NCL), inhibiting translation and reducing antigen presentation to enable immune evasion. Its formation is length-dependent. Ligands like PhenDC3, PyDH2, and PhenDH2 disrupt NCL binding, restoring EBNA1 expression and antigen presentation. The structure prefers U-shaped ligands that stack on G-tetrads. Targeting G4-EBNA1 exposes infected cells to immune response, offering a therapeutic strategy against EBV.
Small_molecule_information: 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide;dibromide, also referred to as PhenDH4, which contains a central phenanthroline core with 1-ethylquinolinium-6-yl lateral groups and demonstrated good G4 binding but no biological effect, incorporates the large, aromatic 1,10-phenanthroline core, a structure well-known in coordination chemistry as a powerful transition metal ion chelator, this core is symmetrically linked to two permanently charged quinolinium fragments, the significance of 1,10-phenanthroline lies in its ability to form a perfect bidentate pocket for metal ions like Fe2 or Cu2, consequently, 2-N,9-N-bis[(E)-(1-ethylquinolin-1-ium-6-yl)methylideneamino]-1,10-phenanthroline-2,9-dicarboxamide;dibromide likely exhibits a dual mechanism of action: it acts as a bis-cationic DNA intercalator and as a metal chelator, potentially disrupting cellular metal homeostasis or generating reactive oxygen species via metal complexes, such molecules are often investigated as highly potent multi-target anticancer agents.

RNA information: TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Its structural feature is that it lacks the characteristic internal loop of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, but contains the UCU bulge structure that binds to HIV-1 Tat. Functionally, it is mainly used as a control in experiments such as SPR. By comparing its binding with target RNA (e.g., ⅡBh), it evaluates the binding specificity of compounds to ⅡBh and assists in distinguishing non-specific binding from specific interactions.; TARh is a hairpin RNA oligonucleotide used as a control. Small_molecule_information: 2-[3-(2-aminoethyl)-5-[2,6-bis(2-aminoethyl)-4-methoxyphenyl]-2-methoxyphenyl]ethanamine (also referred to as the target compound) was synthesized via palladium-catalyzed Suzuki coupling reactions and characterized by methods such as Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) and Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR); functionally, this molecule can bind to the hairpin RNA (Bh) of the HIV-1 RRE subdomain IIB, though a high ligand/Bh molar ratio is required to produce detectable spectral changes and its binding affinity is relatively low, and it is a complex molecule containing multiple primary amine groups (RNH2) and a polyphenol ether structure, whose scaffold is composed of two substituted phenyl rings linked by an ether oxygen, with a total of four 2-aminoethyl side chains and one methoxy group on the rings, and this polyamine structure grants the molecule high cationic character and hydrophilicity at physiological pH, enabling strong interaction with anionic biomolecules, while in practice, polyamine-like molecules are often used as non-viral gene delivery vectors, forming stable complexes with nucleic acids (DNA/RNA) to facilitate transfection, or as modulators of polyamine metabolic pathways, and its high cationic nature also suggests potential antimicrobial or antifungal activity.

This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).; The IRES-A (WT) sequence is capable of forming a stable G-quadruplex structure. This structure is essential for the initiation of cap-independent translation of the human vascular endothelial growth factor (hVEGF).·
Small_molecule_information: N-[2-[4-[4-[2-(diethylamino)ethoxy]anilino]quinazolin-2-yl]phenyl]-3-(4-methylpiperazin-1-yl)propanamide is a quinazoline derivative incorporating a complex N-methylpiperazine substituted propanamide side chain. This structure aims to improve solubility and bioavailability and is viewed as a precursor to a highly active kinase inhibitor for treating cancer or autoimmune diseases.

RNA information: Ca.LSU (Candida albicans Large Subunit) is a self-splicing intron of Class I located in the ribosome large subunit of the Candida albicans 26S rRNA gene. It plays a key role in the maturation process of rRNA, removing itself from the rRNA precursor through the self-splicing mechanism, thereby allowing for further processing and maturation of rRNA. The structure and function of Ca.LSU are of vital importance in the biosynthesis of ribosomes. Its correct folding and catalytic activity are crucial for the maturation of rRNA and the assembly of ribosomes. Studies have shown that certain compounds, such as pentamidine, can inhibit the self-splicing activity of Ca.LSU by binding to specific regions of introns and altering their folding structure. This inhibitory effect can be partially or completely reversed by adding positively charged molecules such as magnesium ions, calcium ions or arginine, which indicates that these positively charged molecules compete with pentamidine for binding sites, protecting the normal folding and function of introns.
Small_molecule_information: 4-[5-(4-carbamimidoylphenoxy)pentoxy]benzenecarboximidamide, also referred to as Compound 24 (EBAB, Pentamidine), is an amidine linked by an alkoxy chain, where two amidine groups are connected by a relatively long pentyloxy ether chain, this molecule is an aromatic diamidine with benzamidine pharmacophore and features a 1,2-ethane/1,5-bis(4-amidinophenoxy)pentane scaffold and bisamidinium groups, its bis-amidine ether structure is highly significant in medicinal chemistry, exemplified by the well-known antiprotozoal drug Pentamidine which acts by binding to parasite DNA, it can also bind r(CUG)exps UU loops/5CUG/3GUC hairpins, displace MBNL1, correct DM1 splicing defects, act as an antibiotic adjuvant against multidrug-resistant bacteria, inhibit Class I intron self-splicing, and has low toxicity, the structural features of the molecule make it a candidate for a potential antiprotozoal or antifungal drug particularly against diseases like Pneumocystis pneumonia, and its activity is closely related to the length and flexibility of the linker chain, while also showing promise for DM1 and drug-resistant infections.

Small_molecule_information: 2-[[6-amino-2-[[2-[(5-methyl-[1]benzofuro[3,2-b]quinolin-5-ium-11-yl)amino]acetyl]amino]hexanoyl]amino]-5-(diaminomethylideneamino)pentanoic acid is a complex quinoline-peptide conjugate where the benzofuroquinoline quaternary ammonium core is linked to an alkaline peptide chain containing lysine and arginine residues. This structure is a targeted drug design: the benzofuroquinoline acts as the cytotoxic warhead, and the alkaline peptide acts as a targeting vehicle to enhance cellular uptake and nuclear localization in anticancer drug development.

RNA information: A distinct class of RNAs containing 1×1 nucleotide internal loops within expanded trinucleotide repeats has been implicated in trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders (TREDs), a group comprising over 20 neurodegenerative and neuromuscular disorders.
Small_molecule_information: 3,5,7-trihydroxy-2-(3,4,5-trihydroxyphenyl)chromen-4-one, also referred to as Myricetin, is a natural flavonol compound with the formula C15H10O8, and is a flavonoid with antioxidant, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects that binds to CAG repeat RNA, inhibiting toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Its structure is a flavonol backbone with hydroxyl groups on the A ring (5 and 7) and C ring (3), and crucially, three hydroxyl groups on the B ring (3, 4, and 5 positions, a pyrogallol moiety), and its polyphenolic structure enables metal chelation and free radical scavenging. Myricetin is widely found in fruits, vegetables, berries, wine, and tea. Functionally, possessing six hydroxyl groups, particularly the pyrogallol structure on the B ring, Myricetin is recognized as one of the most powerful natural antioxidants known, capable of efficiently scavenging various free radicals and protecting cells from oxidative stress, and it also exhibits significant anti-inflammatory, antiviral, antitumor, and cardiovascular protective effects, often by modulating signaling pathways and enzyme activities, in addition to its anticancer and neuroprotective properties linked to its binding with CAG repeat RNA and inhibition of toxic protein aggregation in Huntingtons disease. Due to its potent antioxidant capacity, it is extensively studied and applied in nutritional supplements and natural drug development.
